7,IssueCommentEvent,2016-05-24 20:50:19+00:00,35384742,xi/ldif3,202576,xi,,,153591981,221397090,That is in fact a bigger issue you found here. I had misread the spec and had thought that all values were utf8 encoded. But actually data provided in base64 or via url can be anything. This means that the existing API (everything is converted to unicode strings) is no longer viable.

My idea is to have two modes: The default mode still tries to interpret everything as utf8 and returns bytes on error instead of crashing. This is mostly backwards compatible and should cover most cases.
For the cases where a stricter handling is required there should be a second mode that only returns bytes in the first place.
@davbrink Do you think this would be a viable solution for your usecase?",{},,User,False

33,IssueCommentEvent,2015-07-17 13:08:28+00:00,25983331,fnl/segtok,109501,fnl,,,91676033,122270291,"Ok, I've built a counting tool to look into the issue. Will add it here in a bit. If I count the tokens in the Brown corpus using the continuations used by segtok (*at after and are as at between but by during for from has in into is nor of on or than that though to upon via was were whereas whether while with within*) I get the following result, where Freq. SS is the percentage of times the word was used as a sentence starter:

Particularly the top words are good candidates to remove from the list of continuations. Overall, there seems strong evidence that *during*, *after*, and *but* were not that good choices for continuations, because there is a more than 20% base chance that they get used as a sentence starter. And there is relatively strong evidence for this, too (i.e., more than 500 cases each). However, the word that sparked this discussion - *to* - clearly is a good continuation word (<1% base chance, >40k examples).
I will look at this on a large scientific corpus (PMC OA), too, and see if that would change anything.

- If I would have distributed the data in binary form, you would not have experienced any trouble.

My point is that **somewhere** there must be a repository of original, not parsed (or minimally parsed) data from which all binary flavors (probably HDF5 or/and SQLite) can emerge. 987,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-23 19:46:27+00:00,18342888,CleanCut/green,5838512,CleanCut,,,290539249,359907794,"The actual stdout/stderr emitted during a test can be captured and discarded with `-q, --quiet-stdout`.  If you mean the traceback output from errors/failures, then they occur after the summary of results because each result summary line is output before the test runs colored white (partly so that if a crash in green itself occurs then you can see which test you were on, and partly so that if the test takes a long time to run then you know which test is running while you wait).  Then after the test completes it is rewritten in the appropriate status color and letter code.

Tracebacks and stdout and stderr and skip information is collected as we go.  Once all the tests are run, we then present the traceback, stdout, stderr, and skip information -- which is easy to rearrange or toggle in the code.

You could certainly start storing the summary information as we go along, and then output it again (or only) at the end.  If you _only_ output it at the end, you run the risk of a very large delay with no feedback about what is happening (a lot of people don't like that) and if anything goes wrong and green itself crashes, then you won't know where it was.  I'm not sure that would be useful to many people.  If you repeat the summary output at the end, I suppose some people may find it useful.  But in many cases where there are hundreds or thousands of tests...you will still only see a fraction of your summary on your terminal at a time.  Again, I am not certain that would be a generally useful feature.

Perhaps a more useful feature would be an option to disable outputting the tracebacks?  You can already disable stderr/stdout.  You can already disable skip output.  If you could also disable error/failure tracebacks, then you would be left with the line-per-test output and the final count.

The per-test lines are initially output  [here](https://github.com/CleanCut/green/blob/master/green/result.py#L432) and then re-written with the correct status code and color [here](https://github.com/CleanCut/green/blob/master/green/result.py#L473)

If you wanted to add a new flag, you would do that [here](https://github.com/CleanCut/green/blob/master/green/config.py#L27) and [here](https://github.com/CleanCut/green/blob/master/green/config.py#L78) and [here](https://github.com/CleanCut/green/blob/master/green/config.py#L415).

Also, it is worth mentioning that some people simply use the `-f, --failfast` option so that after any test fails the remainder of the run is aborted, and the one traceback is output. 1556,IssueCommentEvent,2017-08-19 15:14:54+00:00,49008290,dhylands/rshell,127500,dhylands,,,251001077,323529047,"Control-X is basically just an exit to the host side of the REPL. It doesn't send anything to the pyboard (since you may be running a program and you don't want to interrupt it). Control-D is then just an exit to rshell.

When you relaunch rshell, it will try to send some commands to the board. rshell relies on pyboard.py to interrupt the currently runnning program by sending Control-C followed by some control characters to enter the raw-repl.
If I had to guess, I would say that the ESP32 isn't responding to the Control-C's in order to interrupt the currently running program.
So yes, exiting rshell does not leave the board in the same state as a hard reset, and that's by design. It leaves it with whatever program was running, running.",{},,User,False

2436,IssueCommentEvent,2017-02-15 20:42:20+00:00,5934517,dpkp/kafka-python,916148,Drizzt1991,,,207763518,280132545,"So here are my assumptions of what you want to achieve:
* Consume a topic and save position in kafka
* Also save position in Redis in case the consumer goes down the hard way, so we can restart from the same message.
* If the position in redis is obsolete (it was removed for example), we need to load the first available message in the topic.
1) You don't need to call `seek` before every `poll()`. Just call it once during startup.

2) `consumer.position` is the next message to be read. It will be equal to next_offset after you call `consumer.seek(tp, next_offset)`. What you want is probably `consumer.commited(tp)`, it will load last saved offset in kafka.
3) You can reset it with something like:
log.info(""Offset out of range. Seeking to begining"")
The following seem to work ok for me:
# To consume latest messages and auto-commit offsets
# Make a unique offset key for storage
# Get latest offset from redis, if not found, fetch from kafka
log.info(""offset wasn't found in redis, seek from kafka"")
log.info(""Offset out of range. Seeking to begining"")
# You can save `consumer.position(tp)` to redis after this,
# but it will be saved after next message anyway

2722,IssueCommentEvent,2015-07-15 12:41:17+00:00,22117429,rmjarvis/TreeCorr,3419552,suchyta1,,,46249316,121603866,"Working on the Balrog paper, I've set up some wrappers to do automatic jackknifing with TreeCorr. I'm not saying what I have is the ideal interface, but I figured I'd share some thoughts that might be useful.

I do all my jackknifing with Erin Sheldon's k-means, and there's two options of how to specify the regions. Either you give it N, the number of regions to generate, or you give it a file that you've generated yourself, to read the k-means centers. I think something like this allows a reasonable range of functionality, at least as far as k-means is concerned.

Also, something which I find useful, is to be able to return the full vector of correlation functions over the jackknife realizations, in addition to just the covariance and the ""full region"" results. So I think it'd be nice to at least optionally return this object if you specify some keyword.
I can share code/ideas if that's useful, and would even be happy to contribute to the repository if useful. (...After I'm done writing my thesis.)",{},,User,False

3493,IssueCommentEvent,2018-04-26 05:31:44+00:00,106929485,ikalchev/HAP-python,25887324,ikalchev,,,314891628,384518420,Basically I see two use cases for how the value is retrieved - you retrieve a cached value or get it anew, where the latter is achieved with setting the getter_callback. Your point about regularly refreshing the value falls into the first case and can be achieved using the already established approach with the `Accessory.run` method.

Having said that, I think it is flexible enough to have a `get_value` method (because the property does not make it obvious that there could be delays) and an optional `getter_callback` which is called within it. In this way, we leave it to the user to choose the drawback most suitable for her -(1) increased latency in `to_HAP` because of the `getter_callback`, but minimum interaction with the ""device"" or (2) fast `to_HAP`, because of no `getter_callback`, but increased overhead of regularly updating the value.

From the set of sensors I have, there are some which have limited lifetime and these should be ""read from"" as rarely as possible. Thus, I have a 20/30 min `run_at_interval` for them currently and would pretty much like a `getter_callback`. However, for others I have a 1/2 min interval which works fine as it is.

3519,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-06 07:36:17+00:00,31665258,jonathanj/eliottree,112166,jonathanj,,,271161291,342068245,"Eric, some comprehensive documentation for this feature would be great! In fact #37 has been open for a while. Documentation like this seems like it ought to be in eliottree rather than Eliot, however Eliot has a well structured documentation resource already. I'm not personally too phased about this since I'd appreciate the documentation either way, it might be worth running it past Itamar on the Eliot issue. Nevertheless, I appreciate your effort and enthusiasm, thank you!
Recently-ish I ditched my own tree parser implementation in favour of the stricter one in Eliot, it's not impossible that subtle changes went unnoticed. Let me address some of your points:

1. The way the filtering works now, it throws away task dictionaries from the input before building the tree, so when Eliot parses them they're not there. [What happens in Eliot's parser](https://github.com/ScatterHQ/eliot/blob/master/eliot/_parse.py#L56) is that any tree with any incomplete child tasks is itself considered incomplete. [These are attached](https://github.com/jonathanj/eliottree/blob/master/eliottree/_parse.py#L26) to the end of the output, since they won't ever appear in the `parser.add` result. The order of these is not well-specified. There are probably two separate considerations here:
* Investigate the possibility of filtering the parsed tree to avoid intentionally creating incomplete trees.
* Outputting a header like ""Incomplete tasks"" for truly incomplete tasks.
2. I'm reasonably sure that this is a regression and that it used to work the way the documentation describes. Do you have a preferred mode of operation? I can see a benefit for each case:
* Filter subtasks from the tree in order to cut down noise. (Current behaviour.)
* Select whole tasks where any sub task matches the expression. (Previous behaviour.)

3. The `&&` behaviour in jmespath is fairly new. It was introduced in 0.9.0 and eliottree depends on 0.7.1 or greater. Having said that, it definitely looks like multiple selects behaviour is broken. Looking at the code it would appear that they're not being properly combined.
* Filter/select tasks after parsing all the original input, instead of before.
* Clearly separate the listing of complete and incomplete tasks, possibly also sorting the incomplete tasks.
* Implementing `--filter` and `--select` as mutually exclusive options is a good change, since there is a use for both modes of operation
* `--select` is confusing is because I conflated the concept of filtering and selecting in a few parts of the implementation.
* Bump the jmespath dependency and stop supporting multiple `--select` arguments.
I'd be keen to hear your thoughts.",{},OWNER,User,False

3558,IssueCommentEvent,2017-08-27 04:47:36+00:00,17003578,lingthio/Flask-User,4627492,lingthio,,,45667847,325176935,OK. Good news and Bad news:

Using your example code for MongoAlchemy, I managed to get a MongoAlchemyDbAdapter up and running for most functionalities. Mongo ObjectIDs can be retrieved as a hex string, which can be converted to a Python Integer. There were a lot of places where Flask-User just changes an object attribute, which needed to be replaced with a call to db_adapter.update_object():

These object Id integers have more than 16 digits, which the current token manager can not handle (it assumed integers were less than 16 digits). In v0.9 of Flask-User I was able to make the token manager handle unlimited length integers, but this breaks backward compatibility for v0.6.

Another issue that needs to be resolved is that Flask-User currently relies on User.roles for role-based authentication, implemented as a UserRoles pivot table. This needs to be reworked so that we can use a ListField in MongoAlchemy.
I will continue to work on this in the v0.9 branch of the code, and will update this issue if progress is made.

3616,IssueCommentEvent,2018-08-28 10:44:54+00:00,44303047,moses-palmer/pynput,15170036,RedFantom,,,350934016,416538149,Okay, so I think we still don't really understand each other.

Immediate propagation to the main thread would be nice, but would indeed require an API breaking change. What I am proposing is simply allowing the user the option to not have the Listener catch all the exceptions in its own Thread for propagation to the main thread in the first place.

Exceptions in separate threads simply have their tracebacks printed to stderr and then crash the Listener, so there is immediate feedback. Because all exceptions in the callbacks are caught by default, escaping this `try/except` block requires quite some additional code for all listeners. It would be more efficient (both in terms of amount of code and in terms of speed) to simply provide the option to skip the handling of exceptions, like [this](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/compare/master...RedFantom:master).

My initial question concerned the execution of the code. I had expected errors caught in the Listener callbacks to be printed to `stderr` by default because there is a `raise` at the end of the `try/except` block, but it is never executed. That is what really confused me.

A simple option that disables the handling of exceptions makes it much easier to debug errors that occur in the listeners. Making them propagate to the main thread would be the best solution in general, but I do not really see a way to do that properly",{},NONE,User,False

3757,IssueCommentEvent,2017-09-01 07:22:11+00:00,95915008,vrasneur/pyfasttext,8555503,vrasneur,,,254508805,326509475,"The issue you are referring to (https://github.com/salestock/fastText.py/issues/125) is a specific problem with the other binding `fasText.py`.
`fastText.py` is not able to load models generated by the newest versions of FastText, because Facebook engineers have changed the model format.

* you are trying to load an invalid (corrupted) model.

4891,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-28 12:17:52+00:00,60546709,nithinmurali/pygsheets,14262536,Kordishal,,,309061855,376865911,">The major reason drive api acess is not optional is because it it used for mapping ssheet name to ssheet key, which i think is the most used feature. But yeah if someone is not using those, we can give an option.

Yes by default Drive API will be used. The drive API is necessary for exporting, deleting, listing sheets, all permission related stuff (and if they are implemented comments). So it would be hard to separate and making it clear which functionally works without Drive API access.

Another difference is, that the Drive API does not have a Quota as limiting as Sheets API. It is like 1'000'000 API calls a day. So if we want to implement #143 we only need to count API calls to the Sheets API.
>  If we are writing a wraper for Drive Api shouldn't we analogously write an wrapper for Sheets api too ? Especially as you are placing the wrapper in api/.
That is the plan yes. But I think doing both at the same time is not a good idea.
> I am not really sure is this will clean the client ? because we still have to define all the functions in the client. As currently client is the only interface between the api and models.

Basically the client would have a drive attribute and a service/sheetapi attribute which would expose the functions defined there. These would be used through the client by the models. But the user does not need most of them, unless they want to use unsupported/unimplemented functionality.

The client only really needs to expose `open`, `open_by_key`, `open_by_url`, `open_all`, `list_ssheets` and `create`. All other functionality is offered by the sheets/worksheets themselves and the functions in client are only go betweens which implement the requests. (The exception would be `get_range` which has the potential to offer direct data access without having to load an entire spreadsheet. This would be useful for people who only use google sheets as read only data source (like configs)).
If we have a SheetsAPIWrapper all `sh_append` functions would be implemented there and accessed by the Models through `self.client.sheetapi` (which would be an instance of SheetsAPIWrapper).
This way the client would be used as a starting point into the spreadsheets, but then the models are used for actual operations on the data.
And if someone really needs to use a functionality which is not (yet) offered by the models they can do so by using the `client.sheetapi.service` which would expose the full googleapiclient API.
The primary advantage would be to have an easier time to maintain this package since the functionalities would be clearly separated. Or do you think a lot of people use `sh_append` directly?

,{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

I have fixed this and pushed the fix to PyPI in version `0.5.0.3` for other users.

However, the fix *for you* may not be as straightforward. First off, install version `0.5.0.3` from PyPI (`pip install --upgrade django-cities==0.5.0.3`). Then run `python manage.py showmigrations cities` to list all migrations for this app.
* If migration `0009` was *not* successfully applied (eg: has an `[ ]`), you should be able to simply run migrations (`python manage.py migrate cities`), then rerun the import script as normal.

* If migration `0009` was successfully applied (eg: has an `[X]`), you'll need to manually drop the `NOT NULL` constraint on the appropriate slug columns in your database. Then you can rerun the import script as normal.
Post back here if you have any further issues or questions with/about this.
Closing, but feel free to reopen if it's not fixed.",{},,User,False

5603,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-31 18:03:38+00:00,83844720,ageitgey/face_recognition,896692,ageitgey,,,293243102,362018429,"Sure. On [this line](https://github.com/ageitgey/face_recognition/blob/master/examples/facerec_from_webcam.py#L40) of the standard webcam demo, you can change this line:

That will make it look for smaller/further away faces at the expense of running slower by upsampling the original image more. With that setting, I can easily find tiny faces across the room with my computer's webcam.

Note that if you are running the ""faster"" version of webcam demo, it is down-scaling the image to 1/4 size to speed things up. That will make it a lot worse at finding far away faces. So that particular demo isn't the best if you care about finding all possible faces in the image.

5786,IssueCommentEvent,2016-05-20 09:19:14+00:00,34016445,peterbrittain/asciimatics,4748877,peterbrittain,,,133700460,220557107,"The exact interface here might be interesting.  `Screen.wrapper()` does not actually start the event loop.  It is similar to `curses.wrapper()` in that it notes down the current environment, sets up what it needs and returns the `Screen` object through the function specified as a parameter to the wrapper() function, then tidies up the environment when that function returns.
I guess these days, there might be a case for doing this within a [context manager](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0343/), but that still doesn't solve the scheduling issue.

I would expect applications that want more control on the scheduling to need a method (let's call it `Screen.next_frame()`) that allows them to handle any input and draw the next frame of the `Scene`.  In other words provide a single iteration of what is happening inside `Screen.play()`.  This can then be called at a convenient point inside your main loop.",{},,User,False

am already using it with sync functions.

mostly matching my bit of the docs: ""If your application revolves solely around libusb and does not need to handle other event sources, you can have a program structure as follows:""
In essence that is what my suggestion does, by using a dedicated thread,

The other option described there with all the pollfd stuff + timeout-handling etc.,

select() / poll() using event handling mechanism.

be thread-safe. Also if you're running on a platform which does not have
some sort of timerfd this implementation will drop your transfer timeout
granularity to 60 seconds (you can make this better by using
As for your deadlock you may be hitting an issue introduced in 1.0.20, for

5880,IssueCommentEvent,2017-07-24 12:24:31+00:00,43226304,CodeReclaimers/neat-python,5720083,bennr01,,,244928978,317405846,"@d0pa The main advantage of using `multiprocessing` instead of `mpi4py` is that `multiprocessing` does not need any other dependencies. Most systems which have python installed also have the `multiprocessing` module available. `mpi4py` can easily be installed using `pip`, but it seems like it needs some dependencies (`libmpich-dev`). I heard that some of the universities make it rather hard to get the root access which may be required to install these dependencies.

The second advantage is that it seems like `mpi4py` requires more code. When comparing `evolve-feedforward-distributed.py` and `xor-feedforward-mpi-data-scatter.py` (from https://github.com/CodeReclaimers/neat-python/pull/72/files), `evolve-feedforard-distributed.py` has 85 lines of code less than `xor-feedforward-mpi-data-scatter.py` (however, `xor-feedforward-mpi-data-scatter.py` has a longer docstring and `evolve-feedforward-distributed.py` commandline argument support for testing purposes, so this is not an ideal measurement.).

I would like to say that the third advantage is the easier integration into existing code, but this varies between users. Adding support for clusters using `distributed` requires 3 additional lines of code and a modification to the line containing the `population.run` call. However, if someone writes an evaluation function which actually requires being run on a cluster, that person would have written his/her code with this in mind, so this is not really an advantage. Most people using clusters will have more experience with `mpi4py` than with `distributed`/`multiprocessing`, which makes it easier for them to use `mpi4py`. `neat.distributed` was written for inexperienced people who need to run some evaluations on a cluster without much technical knowledge.

Obviously, there are also some disadvantages of using `multiprocessing` instead of `mpi4py`. One being the Xeon PHI cards (and other technology requiring `mpi`). The probably biggest disadvantage is the performance. I have not measured it, but `mpi` is probably faster than `multiprocessing.managers`. But if data transfer rate between the nodes really matters in comparsion with the evaluation time, then the evaluation would probably be faster when using `neat.parallel` instead of a cluster, wouldn't it?

The reason i chose to use `multiprocessing.managers` instead of `mpi4py` was not related to the advantages and disadvantages. When i looked for a way to run `neat-python` on a cluster, i stumbled upon your PR #72. The PR was closed and not merged, so i thought there was some reason it was not merged. I do not understand most of the MPI code in in your MPI example, so i wanted to either try to split most of the code into a separate module for easier re-usability or create a completely new module. Like i said, because the PR was closed, i thought that there was some reason it was not merged and concluded that using `mpi4py` may not be the best idea, so i did not even try it.
Thanks for offering your help in both this issue and in #51. My cluster is actually already setup, the only issue is that `pip install` requires more time than it should.

@drallensmith The *real* manager and all objects it holds are stored in a seperate child process (according to the documentation of `multiprocessing`). Due to this, both the primary and secondary nodes are client connected to the process holding the manager. When we try to get an element from the queue, the manager process will request the element. If the queue raises `queue.Empty`, the exception is caught and serialized. The client which requested the element creates a new Exception from the serialized data. This new Exception *may* not be of the same type as the original exception, but instead a `RemoteError` containing the error message and the traceback.

I just took a look at your newest commit to config_work. I am not sure why the code blocks, but the secondary nodes first receive the stop value and then put it back into the queue. Maybe this leads to some problems, as the stop value put back into the queue may not the same the primary node put into the queue.",{},,User,False

6119,IssueCommentEvent,2015-03-17 08:56:26+00:00,29258660,etianen/django-python3-ldap,148744,etianen,,,62218917,82211364,"LDAP is an extremely overcomplicated, opaque and generally ridiculous was of storing information. I hate it so much! I wrote this library because I *needed* to interface with an LDAP server, not because I *wanted* to! So don’t expect any dealings with an LDAP server to be easy! :P I once spent a whole day trying to get MediaWiki to play nicely with LDAP authentication!
I take it you’re familiar with configuring a django project? Assuming yes, here’s a bit more depth about the settings:

Entires in an LDAP server are stored in a nested tree structure. This string describes the path to the node where people are stored. In this case com -> example -> people. Typically, for a company whose domain is example.com, the “people” entries would be stored there, but different LDAP servers may be set up differently. Have a browse of your LDAP hierarchy, and see how yours is set up.

All entries in an LDAP server have one or more “classes”, which specify what fields they have and in what form the values are stored. Most LDAP servers use the inotOrgPerson to represent a user. Check what object class is used in your LDAP server to specify a user.
A mapping of django model field names to field names on the user entries in the LDAP server. The defaults should work with inetOrgPerson.

On MySQL,  database is equivalent to schema: one database can only have one schema,
so ```SHOW schemas``` is the same as ```SHOW databases```
The SQLA Dialect is therefore straightforward for MySQL:

On Postgresql, one database can support multi schemas, then each schema has table: by default the schema is called *public* and can be omitted in the query. The schema can be see as a namespace/folder to organize tables.
The SQLA Dialect is implemented using the pg_namespace catalog located in the pg_catalog schema.
You can read more details on the official doc: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/ddl-schemas.html
Now in our case Clickhouse is not multi schemas so it behave like MySQL: one database has one schema.

Clickhouse has no schema awareness, the only namespace-like function we can achieve is by using databases (just like MySQL): That's why as in MySQL Dialect, the Clickhouse Dialect should return the databases as schemas.

SQLA goal is to provide an abstraction layer on top of multiple SQL DB, some with multi schemas, some without: they had to have the notion of schemas, but here we have to take the shortcut schema == database because that's the Clickhouse logic.

6919,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-08 08:32:03+00:00,34864402,apache/incubator-mxnet,2626883,szha,47359,apache,286642162,355907094,"The problem is that in your inference loop there was no blocking operator, which means the next iteration will start before the current iteration finishes. Since the execution for operators are asynchronous, the unfinished operators would accumulate, which results in ever-increasing memory usage.

The quickest fix is to print the loss as well in the inner loop. Because the loss is an ndarray, its `__str__` requires `asnumpy` call, which is blocking. This ensures that one iteration finishes before next could start.",{},MEMBER,User,False

7156,IssueCommentEvent,2017-08-11 00:34:05+00:00,64772504,Yelp/py_zipkin,64215,adriancole,49071,Yelp,249475279,321708582,"I am not sure of the log correlation practice in python, but in java we
keep spans regardless of sampling so that log files (not in zipkin) can be
tokenized against a consistent ID. This means when you have a problem you
can log correlate even if unlucky with trace sampling.
So depends on if there are any such non-tracing consumers of these ids.
Consider a case where the root span has a very low sampling rate and there
are a bunch of nested zipkin spans.
If the trace is not sampled, the root span's is_sampled flag will be false,
and the span will not be logged. However we are still generating a new span
after entering each zipkin_span context and pushing the span onto thread
This newly generated span is not used for logging (since is_sampled is
false) and there is really no need to push and pop it from the stack. The
total time to generate/push/pop this span from thread local is not very
significant, but this adds up for a service with many nested spans.
Can we do an additional check for whether a span is sampled before
generating its parameters and pushing it onto thread_local?
You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.

7486,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-01 12:24:25+00:00,60273704,mozilla/DeepSpeech,477142,reuben,131524,mozilla,301066103,369575639,This API should be enough to implement the [Web Speech API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Speech_API) with some limitations (ordered by decreasing difficulty to solve):
1. Interim results aren't supported, only a single, final transcription.
`continuous=false` is easy to solve, the Web Speech implementation can apply VAD to the input and finish the inference and match the results against a grammar.
`continuous=true` with a grammar is more complicated, it would require changes to the decoding algorithm.

Interim results are easy to solve, although expensive (latency) with our current setup. We can just add a ""resultSoFar()"" method that decodes the currently accumulated logits. A streaming implementation of the decoder would make this less costly.
`maxAlternatives` is the `top_paths` CTC parameter. Supporting it is easy.",{},MEMBER,User,False

7643,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-16 14:11:26+00:00,70578420,mozilla/mozilla-django-oidc,26739,peterbe,131524,mozilla,232934580,373725113,This issue has gotten out of hand. Let's reign it back in.
If you use the ""code grant"" (i.e. the OIDC server redirects to your *server* with a code) then `mozilla-django-oidc` is it and it's wonderful.

If you want to use the ""implicit grant"" (i.e. single page app without a Django server to send a code to) then you won't be using `mozilla-django-oidc` and it's technically a non-issue for this project.

If you do have a Django server to do the initial authentication but then almost all the other stuff you do in the site is via XHR then `mozilla-django-oidc` is quite clunky. The reason is [this](https://github.com/mozilla/mozilla-django-oidc/blob/master/mozilla_django_oidc/middleware.py#L81). The ""excuse"" is that it's hard to deal with redirects in XHR requests. E.g.
And challenge is that `fetch()` will follow redirects. True for basically any 3xx error.

Ultimately I think we make a special case for `request.is_ajax()`. If that's the case, and the middleware deems it time to do a session refresh, then it should yield a 403. Why? Because the security of the server wants to say ""I don't want to serve you. But if you mind going to <url> to refresh I'd be willing to try again.""
Then, the ajax-y sites can watch out for that. E.g.
// This could be made a lot fancier.
alert('You have to refresh your authentication session. Going to redirect you.')

,{},COLLABORATOR,User,False

element of each array holds information about the node `i`. Node 0 is the
tree's root. You can find a detailed description of all arrays in
`_tree.pxd`. NOTE: Some of the arrays only apply to either leaves or split
nodes, resp. In this case the values of nodes of the other type are
The number of nodes (internal nodes + leaves) in the tree.
The current capacity (i.e., size) of the arrays, which is at least as
The maximal depth of the tree.
children_left[i] holds the node id of the left child of node i.
children_left[i] > i. This child handles the case where
children_right[i] holds the node id of the right child of node i.
children_right[i] > i. This child handles the case where
feature[i] holds the feature to split on, for the internal node i.
threshold[i] holds the threshold for the internal node i.
value : array of double, shape [node_count, n_outputs, max_n_classes]
Contains the constant prediction value of each node.
impurity[i] holds the impurity (i.e., the value of the splitting

8051,IssueCommentEvent,2016-09-28 18:51:45+00:00,24774658,boto/boto3,4605355,kyleknap,327752,boto,178900827,250263377,"Ah, I see what is happening. All that info you provided helped a bunch.

So this is what is happening. So in the switch to 1.4.0 we swapped out the s3 managed transfer implementation for a more powerful, unified implementation in pulling in ``s3transfer``. When the ``download_file`` is created we create a brand new ``S3Transfer`` object each [time](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/boto3/s3/inject.py#L122). When we create a new ``S3Transfer`` object we actually create a ``s3transfer.manager.TransferManager`` under the hood and we create a new one every time. The ``TransferManager`` [registers new handlers to deal with an issue with progress callbacks for uploads](https://github.com/boto/s3transfer/blob/develop/s3transfer/manager.py#L471-L479), but the issue is we are creating a new ``TransferManager`` every time and as a result we register those two every time as well.
I think what needs to happen the following needs to happen:
1) In the ``shutdown()`` method for ``TransferManager``, unregister those two events.
2) Call ``shutdown()`` in the client methods to make sure they events get cleaned up.
As to workarounds for right now, you can use ``S3Transfer`` directly. So you should be able to do something like this:
Let us know if that helps. In the meantime, we will look into fixing this.",{},,User,False

8058,IssueCommentEvent,2015-12-16 00:05:13+00:00,6670942,boto/botocore,2643092,JordonPhillips,327752,boto,122372547,164940749,"@jamesls There are several events in `Endpoint` that `before-call` would trample if that were used. I didn't want to interfere with anything else that we (or a customer) might be doing. Further, intercepting at that point allows for the possibility of testing out retry logic. As far as `Response` is concerned, how about letting them pass in the status code and reason of the response, and we create it for them? Or maybe even a `ClientError`?

1. I would be +1 on something like this. We could potentially have a map of requests to responses and the user would pass in the history size they want on creation of the `Stubber` class (probably defaulting to 1).
2. Right now you can stub errors by passing in an error response. I think letting them pass a status code / reason would probably suffice though.
3. I think these would be cases for subclasses. We could encourage this by making `Stubber` an abstract class and providing some number of implementations.

4. I think this could potentially be useful, but it skirts around validation. Additionally, they would need to provide this for every single call where they want their function to run, and I'm not sure that's a wonderful interface.

@garnaat in the current state, there's not really a better way to go about it since you can't short-circuit `Endpoint`. With this PR you would be able to short-circuit with an event handler.",{},,User,False

External network can be namesake to an orgvdc network, so having a global delete command viz.
vcd network delete <network-name> without specifying the type is not feasible. And if we have to specify the type of network, then we are better off using the sub command pattern.
External networks are in a different league than org vdc networks. I don't see a reason to list them together. Also, the implementation will be murky if we try to bunch them up, since
1. external networks are only accessible by sys admins, i.e. our implementation will need to have user centric logic or else any non sys admin will get exception while listing networks
2. there is no direct query to list external networks and orgvdc networks in one shot, i.e. we will have to call two different functions and combine their outputs.
I think we should keep the sub-command scheme to work with different types of networks

,{},MEMBER,User,False

8388,IssueCommentEvent,2015-07-08 09:22:14+00:00,25490273,arteria/django-compat,62745,spookylukey,554194,arteria,88047190,119516212,"Can I point out that this issue is a major data loss bug?
If you get `get_queryset` wrong, then Django's dynamic `RelatedManager` stops working.
This is shown by the failing tests in my branch: https://github.com/arteria/django-compat/compare/master...spookylukey:get_query_set_patch_bug

In my example, `house.rooms.downstairs()` starts returning `Room.objects.downstairs()` instead of just the rooms that are related to the house. So if you do `house.rooms.downstairs().delete()`, you just deleted **far** more things than you intended to (Similarly an `update()` method affects far more records than intended).
Please fix this bug, by removing the `get_query_set` monkey patching. That method can't work, since it can't monkey patch the `RelatedManager` classes, which are dynamically created.",{},,User,False

8469,IssueCommentEvent,2017-08-02 17:41:33+00:00,2625205,robotframework/Selenium2Library,2665023,aaltat,574284,robotframework,243481792,319745392,"Yes, me requesting a PR from you, means that you would also provide the code. But it's not mandatory, submitting issues and providing ideas how to enhance the library is great too.

If you don't want to provide the implementation, then it waits for someone else to provide the implementation. Currently I am focused to fix the final things for the next release [1] and I not taking anything else for development in the near future. Then the other option is you to find someone else to provide the implementation. You can either try find someone, who is facing the same problem and ask him to provide the implementation or you can pay someone to provide the implementation.

But if you know basics of Python and selenium then making the implementation should be relatively easy. The Robot Framework library API is explained in the user guide[2] and there are plenty of keywords where you can take an examples. The Selenium2Library uses the the dynamic library API[3], but it's invisible when creating new keywords (only thing which is needed, is to use `@keyword` decorator before the method definition).

8696,IssueCommentEvent,2016-08-20 17:21:08+00:00,54980593,pypa/setuptools,1026649,ncoghlan,647025,pypa,172145715,241212548,"Changing the default sdist output format in distutils for 3.6 would definitely be possible (albeit needing to be done before the first beta next month), as the standard library has `tarfile` and `gzip` support, and the `shutil` archive operations abstract away the format specific details for basic operations: https://docs.python.org/3/library/shutil.html#archiving-operations

For sdist, I think `tar.gz` makes the most sense as the default format, as it really is intended as just a release archive, with it being converted to another format (install directory, wheel, egg, or a downstream format) prior to actual use. Wearing my ""Linux distro contributor"" hat, I'll also note that there's a *lot* of existing open source infrastructure tooling built around the notion of upstream projects publishing tarballs as the base unit of a release (git commit hashes are starting to be an acceptable alternative, but tarballs still have a lot of very nice features for the purpose).

I think zip is a better fit for our *built* formats though, so it makes sense to continue to require that for both wheels and eggs (which is already explicitly the case for wheels, and implicitly the case for eggs).",{},,User,False

9789,IssueCommentEvent,2017-04-05 21:22:54+00:00,82729529,google/python-fire,119472,nealmcb,1342004,google,216182118,292001325,"I think using fire with modules will be the most common usage, so I would lean toward @jtratner's notion that not requiring a `-m` option for it is the most helpful simplification. I guess it's ok to also not require a `-f` option, and also try to interpret the first argument at a file.  But while Python files do nearly always have a `.py` extension, directory names can be loaded the same way, interpreted as modules (and often named the same).  So we do have to be careful about which to make the default.  I guess it's more obvious to the user how to give a fully-qualified file name to make it obvious that they want a file loaded, so my first notion is:
1. try to load it as a module name via imp.find_module
2. try to load module as a file or directory name via imp.load_source

I've left out trying to interpret the first argument as a class or method, since it seems more complex to specify syntax for those names than to use the existing fire approach to accessing those. But I'm open to examples that motivate adding that.
I've made a first shot at implementing the above in https://github.com/nealmcb/python-fire/tree/issue29-fire-without-edits
and the main issue I see is that error handling may be confusing, since we're not exactly sure what the user intended.
I'm happy to either add functionality as we go along via pull requests to the google branch, or playing for a while in my own repo.",{},,User,False

* Data format: There main problem we need to solve is that this PR uses `pandas.Panel` which is deprecated (https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/dsintro.html#panel). Thus, at a minimum we should change the Panels to multiindex dataframes. Once we have done this we could go one step further and convert all data to long (I realize this is a major refactor) where dt and sid are the index and columns are position, sector, cap, momentum, reversal, shares_held, energy etc. In that world we only have a single data structure to be passed internally, the risk tear sheet could check for the existence of certain columns and plotting functions extract a list of columns they want to plot. We have ultimately switched to the same format in `alphalens` under a much higher cost because we have done so much later in the game, but it definitely was worth it, as this format is also much easier to extend. I also think we will want to do something similar for the risk model output as well as for the PnL attribution, so the earlier we bite the bullet the better.

# id, type, x, y, z, r, p
Which is one soma, one dendrite with a single segment.
if you compute the length of the neurite `neuron.neurites[0].length` you get 0 instead of 1.
To have the correct representation (right plot), we need to internally duplicate the neurite parent while keeping the child radius.
# id, type, x, y, z, r, p
2 3 0 0 0 2. 1 <-- line 1 duplicated, type changed 1->3, radius changed 1.->2.
Now you get a length of 2 and a volume of 12.56

This is not an issue with FA

`<a>` tags have the default display property set to `inline`. Since you are inserting a font awesome icon with a `display: inline-block` inside an anchor tag, you should edit your `a` tags accordingly.
Please note that I do not recommend to set this rule, it is just an example.
You should go for a css class such as `.button` with display property set as `inline-block` and add that class to your button links

10570,IssueCommentEvent,2018-02-12 20:26:06+00:00,73504243,Netflix-Skunkworks/cloudaux,11035926,mikegrima,1728142,Netflix-Skunkworks,296473303,365050985,"Hello @0xdabbad00 : Nothing explicitly linked anywhere. We began noticing some major issues with Security Monkey, where it was effectively crippled.

To resolve, I made a very significant (and very long overdue) refactor to make use of Celery and to segment all watchers into individual processes, and to have those processes terminate when complete to free up available RAM.

We noticed on our other major project [Netflix-Skunkworks/Historical](https://github.com/Netflix-Skunkworks/historical), that we observed similar memory leakage.  @kevgliss did some major investigative work, and tracked down that some where after botocore version 1.7.45(IIRC?), there has been major memory leakage when retaining onto old clients.

Our attempt to permanently resolve this issue is to cache the credentials instead of the boto connections, and to simply create new botocore sessions with the cached credentials each time. We are hopeful that this would help resolve the issue.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

- User debug mode: On by default, but able to be turned off. Checks for things like invalid arguments and mismatched dimensions.
- Developer debug mode: Off by default, turns on things like asserts.

To make it possible to turn off user debug checks, I think we could probably change `DYNET_INVALID_ARG` so that it explicitly performs a check, and throws an error if necessary. If user debug mode is turned off, this macro will instead do nothing.

11020,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-31 00:45:20+00:00,70260962,Tubular/sparkly,7946540,mantzouratos,2808498,Tubular,325932068,393365044,Thanks for the feedback guys! Just to make sure we're all on the same page, the env variable doesn't overwrite _all_ options, just options that the user had already re-defined. So in the following example:
the options passed to spark conf will be `{'a': '1g', 'b': '2g', 'c': '3g'}`.

@skliarpawlo, I'm reluctant to add a special flag to allow one option to take precedence over the other. It sounds inconvenient in the usual case, and in the same vein, should we add a flag as to whether `additional_options` take precedence over `SparklySession.options`? Then, where does this stop? Also, what will the default value of the flag be? Because in 99% of the cases, the user won't change that default, so implicitly it will be imposing some precedence order. As such, I think it's better to just agree on a specific order, document it explicitly and let the rest upon the user. Maybe we should add a warning message?
Warning - option 'a' was defined multiple times, going to use value 'x'.

When it comes to the order proposed here, I prefer to have the env var be stronger than the existing ways because it's very convenient in a usual usecase: try running a job by changing the configuration slightly and see how it does. If the env var takes precedence then you can do such experimental runs without changing any code at all.
> The only problem I can see here, is that Sparkly options override configuration defined in such way

right now this is true for all options defined through a sparkly session, even for the ones from `SparklySession.options` or `additional_options`, right? Do you mean you don't like this pattern any more? Or, you meant that you don't like options defined through the env var proposed here taking precedence over spark conf?
If it's the latter, I like to keep the invariant that all options you define through Sparkly have the same exact treatment. They either all override pyspark conf, or none of them does.
If it's the former, I don't think it's that much of a problem as long as we let the user know explicitly.

To your first point, I think that as a sparkly user, one would like to minimize the # of different configuration files or environment variables that they need to touch. It seems much easier / straightforward to define options like `spark.executor.memory` through `SPARKLY_SESSION_OPTIONS`, and rest assured that they are taken care of, than having to `cat >>` to spark's `default.conf`, which could also impact other projects running off the same spark installation later if you're re-using that cluster and don't remember to clean them up after you're done. Whereas with the env var it's as simple as

Finally, I think that for very default machine config options, setting them once in a `default.conf`, then forgetting about them is great, and the env var shouldn't replace that. But for small changes that are actually different from project to project (e.g., how much memory should I give to my python workers?), it makes sense to define them somewhere close to the respective project. I agree that your python code (`SparklySession`) is not a good place for that, but with the env var proposed here you can do it close to where you deploy the code to the actual cluster. E.g.

11094,IssueCommentEvent,2016-08-25 15:45:29+00:00,16316451,GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python,242750,tseaver,2810941,GoogleCloudPlatform,172176157,242435803,"After some discussion with my similarly-curmudgeonly partner @mcdonc, I'm +0 on the use of [`mock.patch`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html#unittest.mock.patch) instead of our [`_Monkey`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/fd0e6bf7aac1f4373c64d8e76552d395a1dbf279/gcloud/_testing.py#L18) class.  Caveats:
- I don't like the decorator style in general, and would prefer that we use `mock.patch` as a context manager.
- `mock-patch.multiple` is most like our current `_Monkey`, and would be the least disruptive replacement.

I strongly dislike the [`mock.MagicMock`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html#unittest.mock.MagicMock) weirdness, as well as any other features which ""avoid typing"" in tests at the expense of comprehensibility.  I've literally quit contributing to more than one project where `mock` was used with abandon for that reason:  ""Unit tests are no place for cleverness.""",{},,User,False

11519,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-17 19:39:40+00:00,22072746,pantsbuild/pex,46740,stuhood,3065172,pantsbuild,322937443,389984822,"> this should not be a retryable event - and as we can see pex already blows up on it, just post-fetch.

When you get an invalid dist, it's effectively impossible to know why... it could have been corrupted anywhere between being created on some machine, being uploaded to a server, and finally when it is downloaded from the server. Because #1 only addresses the download, it doesn't catch as much of the problem.
In-addition to doing #2, it would be great if there were checksums in place on the server side, but I don't know whether such a standard exists for python dists.
> it's not clear to me what problem we would be solving by just moving that validation to fetch-time vs resolve-time.

It prevents putting something broken in the cache, and allows you to indicate to the user which thing from which url was broken. So it solves the ""opaque error"" issue in the description, and prevents it from recurring when you retry.",{},MEMBER,User,False

>Disambiguation from Snackbars: Snackbars present optional information after an action, such as confirming the discarding of a draft. They often allow a user to undo an action just taken.

For import and export of channels, would we really want them to undo an action/dismiss the snackbar so easily? The process of import export and the weight of its importance is much greater than an smaller micro action such as selecting/unselecting a topic to use for an exam (an example proper use of a snackbar). Import/export is a heavy action, so you would want to use alerts to make the user aware of it's presence and status in the import export flow. Conceptually, snackbars and alerts both provide feedback for the user, but are used in two totally different scopes and should match the weight of the particular action. ",{},,User,False

For me the value of using `git describe` is to determine what exact commit has created that specific build. That has been a recurring problem back in KA Lite, where we don't know what commit was used to produce a build, making it hard to make a bugfix release on top of that. The version file is simply a hack to make sure the version was cached to disk in case `git` or the `.git` folder isn't available (a.k.a. for whl and pex files).
With that said, I'm not too concerned with the incorrect output from `git describe`, as long as it does the right thing when you tag the current `HEAD`.

For the other changes -- I do think that we need a fallback in case the VERSION file isn't present, and it would be ideal if that version fallback isn't too far off the last tag. Having a version checker that errors out in case that does happen, is the most important thing in this PR IMO.

My only concerns are making sure we document the behaviour of how we derive the version (there's a lot of cases now as per our chat), and making all the devs know about said behaviour.",{},,User,False

Sent from my phone, sorry for brevity or typos.
- All advanced sequencing attributes are specified upfront, so create_advanced_sequence can scan the dict to get the attribute names (and thereby the attribute ids).
- It's clear what attribute values each step contains.
- It'd be very easy to map some JSON data to an advanced sequence.

- This may necessitate that we hide niDCPower_CreateAdvancedSequenceStep from the API. The reason is because it may seem strange and misleading to mix calls to that entry point along with the proposed approach.
# If the user was expecting to change source_delay for only this new step,
# it will not have the intended effect, nor will it error. This is a problem
# even for the LV API, but at least we have the flexibility to protect
- Modifying steps would also have a similar problem:
# Changes source delay for all steps, not just the currently active one!
# However, I don't think we can get away with hiding the

- If you are able to map attribute names to attribute ids, you could just pass a list of attribute names rather than a dict to create_advanced_sequence. The user could then configure each step using just the session.",{},NONE,User,False

Which is the reason I think that ideally we (Twisted + Autobahn) find a way to support both (having Autobahn take control and handover to Twisted Web **and** having Twisted Web take control and handover to Autobahn).

@Lukasa That is certainly good (not shuffling bytes through a HTTP2 state-machine), but Crossbar.io needs are: we have to take control, since we want RawSocket on the same port. Plus: we don't care about HTTP2 at all. The performance of Twisted Web on HTTP/1.1 serving static assets is limited by Twisted Web not employing in-memory caching of static assets from files. https://github.com/crossbario/crossbarexamples/tree/master/benchmark/web. Then, HTTP2 handshake is more complex than HTTP1, so WebSocket opening handshakes / sec will likely be slower .. but I haven't measured that. Notes on the ongoing performance comparison with MATLAB, for sparsely-populated frames:
- Number of features found went from 546 to 554. The 2-norm metric used by KDtree ≠ metric in pixellated space. But a ~1% difference is probably acceptable.
- Feature-finding time went from 0.106 s/frame to 0.0517 s/frame (!). MATLAB's current best time is 0.0458 s. A full 10 ms of trackpy's time is still used by pims' `as_grey`.
- The final product *looks* worse with the same parameters. For example, I draw your attention to the lower right corner of each image:
So there are some issues to address, but this is a very successful experiment!",{},,User,False

13199,IssueCommentEvent,2017-02-17 21:25:07+00:00,12235583,elastic/curator,1020383,untergeek,6764390,elastic,208538896,280770552,"That would be because your timestring has an unsupported flag in it (`%-j`).  The supported flags are described [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/fe_timestring.html).  `%j` is supported, but not `%-j`.

In #397, support for `%j` was added.  In #578, the regex map for `%j` was modified to support 1 to 3 digits, effectively making `%j` have a compatible regular expression to one for `%-j`.  I would try using `%j` instead.  I haven't had any follow-ups or complaints after the changes in #578, so I presume it should work for you.

Otherwise, I'd recommend using [`source: creation_date`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/fe_source.html#_creation_date) or [`source: stats_result`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/fe_source.html#_field_stats) instead of the index name.
Also, you should update to the latest version of Curator, 4.2.6.",{},,User,False

13573,IssueCommentEvent,2016-12-19 16:03:58+00:00,1605598,bulletphysics/bullet3,22159799,SeanCurtis-TRI,6955508,bulletphysics,195003159,268002509,"It's difficult for me to accurately assess if we're using ""default"" margins.  We have different behavior based on the the type of shape.
- Sphere and Cylinder: no margins are addressed in anyways.  Only the radius is set.
- We have a ""capsule"" shape which is a `btConvexHull` shape with two points and a margin equal to the desired radius.
- Boxes and dynamic meshes are converted into `btConvexHull` and a margin of at least 1e-9 is applied (possibly larger in some use cases.)
- Static meshes use `btBvhTriangleMeshShape` and likewise have a minimum margin of 1e-9.
- Point clouds - we abuse the `btConvexHull` to quasi-support point clouds by pushing the points into the `btConvexHull` and providing it the same minimal margin of 1e-9.
The query we're doing (where the answer depends on ordering) is using the geometry described above with the minimum margin value of 1e-9.",{},,User,False

13579,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-11 11:05:24+00:00,18031064,hmmlearn/hmmlearn,9663231,flxbe,7037444,hmmlearn,271942589,343657097,"Before starting to fit the data, `n_components` initial clusters for the different states are created. Each cluster will then be further separated into `n_mix` clusters to get a first estimate of the parameters for each gaussian.

It seems like one of the initial clusters only ends up with 2 samples, while at least 5 are required for the second level clustering. It looks like a bug to me. The clustering should acount for a lower bound of members per cluster.

I do not have a lot of experience with clustering, and [`sklearn.cluster.KMeans`](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.cluster.KMeans.html) does not include an option for such a lower bound. Intuitively, the clustering should be separated into two steps:
1. Find the `min_cluster_size` nearest neighbours and assign them to each center.
2. Assign the rest of the datapoints using kmeans.
Ideally, this would only differ from the origianl kmeans result if the lower bound constraint is violated. Maybe such a solution already exists?",{},NONE,User,False

So we need a robust tool. Mypy doesn't support https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0561/ yet unfortunately, thus it cannot be used *right now*. I hope mypy dev team will fix it. Personally I have no time for working on mypy unfortunately. ",{},OWNER,User,False
I think 2 months of support (security bugs only) for an old major version after a new major version is released is sufficient, maybe others need longer?

Personally I think after a major version is released and stable nothing new should be released for that major version except critical operational bugs and security bugs. I should be able to merge and deploy a new minor version once tests pass with no more thinking. New features in minor releases just cause confusion. I guess this is how many projects treat patch releases.

I'm not really sure, personally I watch this repo closely and that plus pyup is sufficient. I started thinking about this after noticing how [django released 2.0.3](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/django-announce/hWfAf2SckL4), I wondered how aiohttp would react to security problem like this or more serious (eg. like the [apache struts problem which caused equifax's downfall](https://qz.com/1073221/the-hackers-who-broke-into-equifax-exploited-a-nine-year-old-security-flaw/)).
Perhaps https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/aio-libs for anything critical and/or urgent is sufficient.",{},MEMBER,User,False

14430,IssueCommentEvent,2016-02-25 16:07:46+00:00,24689871,pythongssapi/python-gssapi,119737,DirectXMan12,8986660,pythongssapi,136155995,188855548,"So the question is: does ""undefined"" mean ""up to the implementation"" or ""in an invalid state"" here?
If it means ""up to the implementation"", it seems to me according to 2744, we can count on:
- if DELEG_FLAG is false, delegated_creds is NULL
- if DELEG_FLAG is true, delegated_creds is *valid*
- DELEG_FLAG will always be either true or false
meaning that it's fine to just check NULL.  My concern is that if we add more complicated logic than ""is it NULL or not"" and miss a valid pointer, we'll have a memory leak, since
> If a credential handle is returned, the associated resources must be released by the application after use with a call to gss_release_cred()
(this phrasing seems to support the case that delegated_creds will always be either NULL or valid).

14431,IssueCommentEvent,2017-08-28 14:22:51+00:00,68926339,CMA-ES/pycma,7316439,nikohansen,9052298,CMA-ES,253236713,325367633,"To my understanding of the code, we still keep control in both cases _from the outside_. That is, there is no arbitrary reset of the seed. The difference is that in case of the rotation `seed=None` means we will not be able to reproduce the rotation. The advantage is that we can set the `numpy` seed for the algorithm but get different rotations by default or if we want to (or fix the rotation if we want to by passing a seed). This is not the case for the shift: if we set the `numpy` seed, we do not produce a changing shift over different simulations ever.

We may contemplate over this, but to me it looks more like a feature than a bug. It suggests that for the shift the actual result is relevant and can be interpreted, while for the rotation it is not (which makes some sense to me). We can try to figure out an interface which provides both options in each transformation.
BTW, the `fitness_transformation.Rotated` class now has a `seed` optional argument which is passed to the rotation and used in the tests of V(k)D-CMA-ES. ",{},,User,False

14657,IssueCommentEvent,2015-11-13 20:55:43+00:00,26516210,letsencrypt/letsencrypt,4983116,rewbycraft,9289019,letsencrypt,116410082,156554125,"There is no error, it's just not binding/listening on ipv6. Basically, it seems to be listening on 0.0.0.0 but not :: (aka, it's listening on all ipv4 ips, but not all ipv6 ips) (I gathered this information with netstat). Now, from taking a cursory glance at the code I can spot why it does this.

Your acme_standalone HTTP01Server class uses six.moves.BaseHTTPServer, which is an alias for the normal python3 class of the same name. (And thus, albeit through some layers of abstraction) is effectively doing the same thing as the commands the manual mode tells you to run. Python is strange among the higher level languages in that there are several places where it doesn't transparently handle ipv6. You have to explicitly tell it to bind to/listen on ipv6.
As an example, here is my modified version of the python SimpleHTTPServer command manual mode has you run (I added some spacing for clarity):

Interestingly enough, it does seem that python implements ""accepting ipv4 over ipv6"". What does this mean? Well, if you run this and then check netstat you'll find it's listening on :::80 (port 80 on ::), which means that technically it's only listening on ipv6. BUT ipv6 has this neat socket option called IPV6_V6ONLY (yes, I'm diving a little into the python internals here). Basically, turning this option *off* (which python does) allows an ipv6 socket that binds to :: (all ips/the ipv6 equivalent to 0.0.0.0) to accept both ipv6 *and* ipv4 connections. The only real difference here is that the ips in use are ipv6 and that ipv4 ips are ""mapped"" to ipv6 ips in a way similar to what NAT64 does. Although it uses ::ffff: as the prefix rather than 64:ff9b:: So both ```` curl http://127.0.0.1/ ```` and ```` curl ""http://\[::1\]:8080/"" ```` work (the \ is because curl is weird like that, you have to escape the [ and ] when entering ipv6 addresses).
Just for reference as to what these ips look like, here's the output I get from the script I posted above when running aformentioned curl commands:
Both calls returned the expected data. (For reference to people who've never seen ipv6 before, ::1 is the ipv6 equivalent of 127.0.0.1. Also, I need a shirt that says ""No place like ::1"".)
I hope this clarifies things a bit/helps.

14746,IssueCommentEvent,2017-09-01 15:07:01+00:00,26876372,dateutil/dateutil,1377457,pganssle,9849410,dateutil,254056065,326605350,"@waynenilsen Thanks for your excellent work on this. I'll look at it soon, but it looks good to my initial once-over.

With regards to the capitalization convention, there were recently some discussions in #431 about the convention - PEP 8 suggests CapWords convention for classes, but we're using lowercase. It doesn't help that CPython uses lowercase for most standard library classes (including all those related to dates). I think there's some movement behind the idea ""we don't have to be inconsistent"" and ""we'll start a slow process of moving towards CapWords"", but this project doesn't really get a lot of attention so it's not like there was a spirited debate.

Before this gets released on PyPI (but not necessarily blocking merging into msater), I'd like to get a general sense of the conventions people use in representing durations like this. I'm a bit surprised that you chose M for minutes and m for months. Parsers should probably be normative about these sorts of things, not positive, and I would be kinda surprised if the convention for expressing durations goes with m == Months, M == minutes... I note that [ISO8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Durations) durations uses `M` for *both* and encodes their meaning positionally.

Are tenors ever expressed out of-order? I think maybe the best thing to do would be to impose a big-endian constraint on strings to be parsed, so that we can resolve ambiguities in that fashion (i.e. you will never see a token for a larger unit of time than one you've already seen). We'll still need some sort of token mapping, so that users can specify whether a string like `1.5M` means ""1.5 months"" or ""1.5 minutes"", but it will be less important.",{},OWNER,User,False

15157,IssueCommentEvent,2017-07-26 04:35:38+00:00,66823715,intel-analytics/BigDL,4495653,qiuxin2012,10941215,intel-analytics,244790255,317945014,"Aha, you are creating `RDD[MiniBatch[Float]]` directly. This is much different from our existing examples. @sethah

In our examples, we create DataSet with raw-data RDD, like `RDD[ByteRecord]` or `RDD[Sample]`. And, we use some transform to transform the raw data to a MiniBatch. So, in our examples, the `dataset.size()` means number of samples, `mini batch size` means number of training samples in each iteration.

But, in your case, you create `DataSet` with `RDD[MiniBatch]` directly. Then your `dataset.size()` is number of MiniBatchs. In each iteration, `DistriOptimizer` will fetch `node number * MiniBatch` for training. The `accumulateCount += nodeNumber * MiniBatch.size()`, is really a bug here, the counting doesn't match. We will fix this.

15258,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-30 18:22:08+00:00,30895522,pvlib/pvlib-python,1385621,mikofski,11037261,pvlib,292111987,361686564,"**CORRECTION** The values for imp, vmp, and pmp are **not** slightly off. The method that is being tested is using a higher convergence criteria, and so it is finding a different MPP that is very, very slightly greater, and most likely below the uncertainty. Back checking the slightly different MPP values into the existing method by calling `i_from_v` and `v_from_i` demonstrate that the existing method and the new method being tested agree.
>>> r_spr_e20_327  # test results with way faster 1st and lambert-w 2nd

So there is a 0.02% difference for both Imp and Vmp which results in a 0.00003% difference in power. How do we know that the Bishop method is correct? If we then take the calculated Imp and Vmp and run it back through the existing `i_from_v(vmp_bishop)` and `v_from_i(imp_bishop)`, then we get agreement. Although this difference is very, very small, it will not pass the test criteria, typ. 1E-5, so I have to use `np.isclose(i_from_v(vmp), imp` and `np.isclose(v_from_i(imp), vmp` to compare them to `bishop88`. But this difference does **not** indicate an error in the existing method, it is an artifact of the different convergence criteria.
...,  # big array of i-v curve points
...,  # big array of i-v curve points

So again super small deltas, only 0.003% for both Imp and Vmp, and 0.0000005% for power, but these differences are still bigger than the tolerance to pass the test, so I have to use the existing `i_from_v` and `v_from_i` methods to compare them, which is good, because getting agreement there proves that the points are on both curves, and that the curves from both methods agree.

Therefore the existing method is **not** wrong, because it does get points from the I-V curve, it's just that the ones it finds are not as close to the max power point because the convergence tolerance is lower, and actually more reasonable given the uncertainty.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

15321,IssueCommentEvent,2018-08-21 22:30:03+00:00,18302786,IDSIA/sacred,1160920,Qwlouse,11296960,IDSIA,349171732,414842448,"Oh, that is interesting. I think you are right, this is because `_stop_heartbeat` does not actually make sure the heartbeat is stopped.

I dislike option 1, because then experiments might never finish if the heartbeat blocks. But we can certainly increase this time and kill it afterwards (option 2). I think up to a minute should be OK. Potentially make it configurable.

I see two more possibilities: Ensure old heartbeats are stopped right before starting a new run. That way we are only killing the heartbeat if we need to start a new one and leave it be otherwise. That would depend on the experiment as the central place for knowing which run is active. In theory `ex.current_run` should do just that, but should be carefully checked for thread-safety if we want to rely on it in this context.

Second: We could make a copy of the observers for each run. That way we would not reuse the same FileStorageObserver and they should be able to do their business without interference. But this bug, might cause other problems than just with the observers, so I'm not sure this is a good solution.

15489,IssueCommentEvent,2017-07-31 10:48:45+00:00,23924195,Lasagne/Lasagne,629706,f0k,11752110,Lasagne,244940958,319033837,"You create the network with an `InputLayer` of shape `(None, 3, 60, 80)`. The `DenseLayer` will expect an input of `conv4.output_shape`, not of `conv4.get_output_shape_for(X.shape)`.

Note that `conv4.get_output_shape_for(X.shape)` computes the shape for passing `X` through `conv4` only. If you want to pass it through the full network, you need `lasagne.layers.get_output_shape(conv4, X.shape)`. But this doesn't help against the fact that `input_shape` must match what you feed to the network later, because otherwise it won't set up the weight matrix in the first dense layer correctly.

Similarly, `encoder.get_output_for(encoder_input)` will pass `encoder_input` through the very last layer of the encoder. You want `lasagne.layers.get_output(encoder, encoder_input)` instead. You can also use `build_encoder(encoder_input)` and then `lasagne.layers.get_output(encoder)`.",{},,User,False

15494,IssueCommentEvent,2015-08-25 16:39:27+00:00,23924195,Lasagne/Lasagne,629706,f0k,11752110,Lasagne,42569940,134665524,"Reopening as this issue came up again on the mailing list: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/lasagne-users/ABiUmAIT-ho

To paraphrase, there are two assumptions in our code and/or user code, one of which we would have to break to support this. The assumptions are: a) Every `self.b` and `self.W` is a shared variable, and b) `self.params` contains `self.b` and `self.W`.

I'd suggest to break the first one, such that when a user supplies a custom expression for a constructor parameter `W`, then that layer's `self.W` will be set to that expression, and that expression will also be used as the key in `self.params`.

Furthermore, both our code and user code relies on `get_all_params()` returning a duplicate-free set of shared variables. I'd suggest to change it to extract all shared variables from the parameter expressions by default. This means when somebody sets `W=T.exp(some_matrix)`, then `some_matrix` will be treated as the actual parameter for most purposes -- i.e., training, regularization, counting parameters, storing and restoring values, and so on. It will also practically inherit all tags the layer gave to `W`.
Sander had the objection that if an expression depends on multiple shared variables, it's unclear whether all of them are meant to be trained, and said:

> maybe we should settle on a convention where people set a certain attribute on a shared variable that should not be trained. Kind of like how we have parameters with 'trainable' tags right now. But that starts to get pretty complex.

My suggestion would be to not bother with this, and just have all shared variables involved in an expression be collected by `get_all_params()`. Keeping some underlying parameters fixed can easily be solved by making them constants instead of shared variables. The only use case that wouldn't be covered would be setting `W` to some expression that involves two shared variables, both of which should be trained, but only one of which should be regularized. I think this is exotic enough to not bother with it for now, following our design goal **Pragmatism**. People can always subclass a layer to accomplish more complex parameterization.

> Then I think we have to make the conceptual difference between the 'virtual' parameter variables (which might be expressions, e.g. self.W and self.b) and the 'real' underlying parameter variables more explicit. Else this could lead to a lot of confusion in the long run. We might also want to split get_all_params() into two separate functions, one that gets the virtual parameters (which may include expressions), and one that gets the real parameters (which are guaranteed to be shared variables).

I'd strongly vote for having `get_all_params()` to return the 'real' parameter variables by default, because that way most existing code would continue to work without changes. This could be realized via a `utils.extract_shared_vars()` helper function called from within `get_all_params()` if `extract_shared=True` is given, so people could use `get_all_params(..., extract_shared=False)` to obtain the 'virtual' parameter variables.
> And as mentioned before, we should have some mechanism to mark variables as non-trainable in that case.

I disagree. Layers give tags to 'virtual' parameters, and those should just apply to all 'real' variables contained within. Everything else is unnecessarily complex for now. (And as I said, there's always `T.constant` if it's just about trainability.)",{},,User,False

15715,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-28 01:44:57+00:00,35872414,infoportugal/wagtail-modeltranslation,2297074,dwasyl,12510303,infoportugal,308760176,376732049,Thanks @dmarcelino, I've done some more debugging and come up with a reason.

To start, way back when, I originally configured a translated `Page` model, but also the `Site` model. This was so I could display both language versions of the `site.name` since on my sites there was a need for an overall site name, not just on the root page. This worked fine with the old model of `wagtail-modeltranslation` as enough of the `Page` functions were overridden and it worked. With the new version it seems to have caused it to break.

The problem is that in [wagtail/core/models.py#L153-L167](https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/blob/master/wagtail/core/models.py#L153-L167) the first line pulls up the cached copy of  `wagtail_site_root_paths`, this returns the previously generated `site` object and last `root_page.url_path`. When the `Site` model is translated, `site.root_page.url_path` displayed a translated slug for the `root` page.

For example, Site A with a root page of `my-site`, the `site.root_page.url_path` would be `/my-site/`, if Site A is included in `modeltranslation`, there could be a separate language slug i.e. `site.root_page.url_path_fr` that equals `/mon-site/`. This is a problem because `models.py` `get_url_parts` checks the `url_path` of a particular page against the Site object's `root_path`. If the `root_page` has multiple slugs and a cached version is referenced this won't part and `get_url_parts` returns None, causing a whole bunch of other things to fail.
Anyway, thanks for the tips on this, but the end result is to make sure all language versions of the `Site`'s root page have the same slug (which is never visible anyway).",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

15732,IssueCommentEvent,2016-07-21 02:01:50+00:00,40328394,angr/angr,5071575,ltfish,12520807,angr,161466767,234136590,"> I have run some analyses with both CFGs and I have seen that one analysis could erase some pieces of information gathered by the other analysis (like the list of functions). Is it better to work on two separates projects to build the two graphs or do you think it is not so serious to build both graphs in the same project?

There is a notion that is not very well documented in angr: `KnowledgeBase`. The list of functions are stored in the knowledge base you pass in to `project.analyses.CFG(kb=xxx)` or `project.analyses.CFGAccurate(kb=xxx)`. If you don't specify the `kb` parameter, it will use the default knowledgebase associated with the project itself. Every time you create a CFG, the function list in its associated knowledgebase will be erased and recreated. Therefore you don't have to create two separate projects, just create two separate knowledge bases and pass them to different analyses, respectively.",{},,User,False
