The easiest way to submit this as a change is to open a pull request against this project. To do that, you first need to fork the project by clicking the ""fork"" button in the top right. Then clone your local copy of the repo with `$ git clone git@github.com:user32000/odo.git`. In that repo, create a new branch with `git checkout -b skiprows-fix`. Here you can make changes to fix the error you are seeing. When you are happy with what you have, run `git status` to see which files need to be committed. use `git add <FILE>` for each file that you want to make changes to. When all the files are added, you can collect the changes into a single commit with `git commit`. This will open your editor where you can write a short message about what this change is for. Then, you can run `git push --set-upstream origin skiprows-fix` to push your new branch to github. Finally, when you navigate to your project you should see a new button which will open a PR for your branch against this project where we can review the changes. Hopefully this helps, also, sorry if you were already familiar with this workflow and were just asking about the particulars of the odo workflow.",{},,User,False

Being a core libpcap developer for many years helps. :-)
> is there a feature request here?

I would, on UN\*Xes, provide an API that uses `pcap_get_selectable_fd()` if available and `pcap_fileno()` otherwise.  Whether it should fail or return `fileno(pcap_file(pcap))` if `pcap_file(pcap)` is non-null is another matter - `select()`/`poll()` on a plain file should always return TRUE, but that means there's no point in doing a `select()` or `poll()` on it unless the program logic makes it harder *not* to do so.

I would, on Windows, *not* provide that API, because there isn't a file descriptor that can be provided that would be usable with WinSock's `select()` call, and would, instead, provide an API that uses `pcap_getevent()`, which returns a `HANDLE` that can be used with `WaitForSingleObject()`, `WaitForSingleObjectEx()`, `WaitForMultipleObjects()`, `WaitForMultipleObjectsEx()`, `MsgWaitForMultipleObjects()`, and `MsgWaitForMultipleObjectsEx()`.",{},,User,False

> If you've submitted jobs with a given application folder on disk I would have assumed that Ganga could make use of this folder until the jobs have finished running.

In Ganga we have the principle that when the job is first submitted, it is independent of changes carried out in the locally checked out code. This includes both changing the code in the checked out area or indeed completely removing it. So if we need to use the users environment at the end of the master job (for merging the XML), then in principle the only ""correct"" thing to do is to unpack the tarball from the shared area and run the code in that environment. A very heavy operation but in principle the only correct one,

15997,IssueCommentEvent,2017-02-17 21:22:10+00:00,31315021,Kinto/kinto,17006,glasserc,13413813,Kinto,208519809,280769891,"It seems like `pserve` had a [massive rewrite upstream](https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid/pull/2805) which breaks us in a couple of ways.

- It seems like moving from optparse to argparse (https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid/pull/2864/) changed the semantics of an argument string like `['pserve', 'config.ini', '--reload', 'http_port=8080']`. For some reason, the argument parser fails to parse this, but it will parse correctly if `--reload` comes either before the INI file or after the variable.

- It seems like the implementation of `pserve` used to re-invoke the same command as `sys.argv`, but now specifically recursively invokes `pserve` from inside a ""monitored"" reloader process. The second (inner) execution then tries to parse `kinto serve` arguments with `pserve` and totally fails.

It seems like we'll have to rewrite our `kinto serve` code to make it work with Pyramid 1.8. Since master is kind of broken right now, I think we should revert the change until someone has time to do that. In the meantime, @gabisurita points out that if you remove `--reload` from the `Makefile`, then everything works OK (but without reloading). We could make that change instead and create an issue to track it.",{},,User,False

In the SDK 2.0, the current master version, projection functionality is offered in the SDK in 3 ways:

1.	Helper functions (in C) inside rsutils (https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/include/librealsense2/rsutil.h#L14) allowing the user to project 2D point to 3D space and vice-versa given depth.
2.	Point-cloud processing-block under processing.hpp that generates 3D points (with texture-coordinates) from depth image
3.	Align processing-block under processing.hpp that generates synthetic depth/color frame from the point-of-view of color/depth imager.

Note that this version of the SDK does not support R200. For R200 support, you have to get the legacy branch. Once in the legacy branch, you should be able to find Projection information here: https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/legacy/doc/projection.md.

16225,IssueCommentEvent,2017-08-02 22:29:22+00:00,43091756,hackingmaterials/matminer,986759,computron,14116185,hackingmaterials,246904845,319816627,Ok let me intervene here

Both solutions are technically workable. Even in the case where different options of a featurizer lead to different citations or implemented_by strings, the new solution would still work as far as I can tell. So it is just a matter of style. I'll just list cons and skip pros for each solution.
- verbose / lengthy. Kind of a pain to scroll around in. Requires extra typing

- works in Python, but style inconsistent with other OOP languages (e.g., you can't do this in Java). A minor point but many programmers will be used to method overloading, thus the proposed replacement just feels ""weird"".

- less ""secure"", i.e., it's not clear that self.labels and self.implemented_by are private and should not be directly accessed or modified but rather should be accessed by the method. I can easily see some casual coders deciding to just directly access the instance variables rather than calling the methods that access them, which is an off-label usage and technically would not be guaranteed to be supported.

- the code itself doesn't define a clear contract of what instance variables need to be set for subclasses. Yes, you can write this in the documentation, but this is not as clear a contract as having the methods to be overloaded as actual base class code. For example, when drawing class diagrams the original solution already makes it clear what is expected to be done from subclasses. You couldn't infer from a class diagram what are the specific instance variables that need to be overloaded in the new proposed solution.

- can lead to messy / disorganized code. In the proposal, the init() of the subclass would be responsible for doing multiple things - i.e., setting a bunch of instance vars that relate to references, a big string with credits, an array with implementers, etc. as well as setting up the parameters of the class itself. For example, some coders might define the ""implemented_by"" right at the beginning of the init(). Others might put it at the end. It's cleaner to have the code related to implementors right near the implemented_by() method. Or take labels() - in some cases, this is multiple lines of code. It's better if those lines of code that are just are part of the feature_labels() function instead of in some random place in __init__().
- (not really a big deal IMO) - *requires* storing implemented_by() and labels() when there is often no need to store these.
Basically, I understand that having many methods feels like extra typing / reading. But there are style reasons to have the extra baggage in there",{},,User,False

I’m inclined to agree: if not _complicated_ per se, it’s certainly verbose and error-prone. We can definitely factor out shared machinery. The main reason that I didn’t do so prior to this commit is just that, as you mention, it’s not yet clear what the right abstraction is, and the wrong abstraction is worse than no abstraction.
Just to put this out there—I don’t think that we need a Flux-like or Redux-like architecture at this time. (But perhaps I could be convinced otherwise.)
> putting this info in the URL
This seems reasonable, especially because pagination layout seems like one of the things that you’d be less annoyed about being lost on reload—as opposed to something like the auto-reload flag.

One thing to note: the store module defined in this change assumes that all changes to the data go through it. That is, the only way to change the pagination limit is to call `setLimit`. It seems reasonable to assume that people won’t manually change `localStorage['TF.TensorBoard.PaginatedView.limit']`, or that if they do then they accept the consequences. However, the URL bar is, by design, user-manipulable state. If we store this info in the URL, then we have to watch for changes there, too, and that adds additional complexity.

Perhaps one step in the right direction would be to let `tf-storage` expose a function for creating a general-purpose listener, like `getInitializer` but Polymer-independent? Or something like that. I’m not quite sure.
> moving settings into the TensorBoard server

Don’t think I agree here. Each user should be able to set a different pagination limit, and change this without requiring frontend interaction. Semantically, too, it seems that the backend should tell the frontend what data to render, and not concern itself with how to render it. (Similarly, storing things like chart domains, x-axis values, etc. on the server seems wrong to me.)

Perhaps what you’re hoping for is some kind of “saved layout” that can be shared among users? For instance, a user sets up some settings and clicks “save,” and the settings are sent to the server; other users can then load the layout, which just changes their local settings.
> Mostly just bringing up future considerations.

,{},MEMBER,User,False

Do you have a pointer to the definition of that macro? Also, if this is fine with you, you could depend on our [latest release](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/releases) if you prefer stability over new features. Are there particular changes that caused you a lot of grief?

There is some randomness in the training process of fastText, due to the use of the [Hogwild algorithm](https://papers.nips.cc/paper/4390-hogwild-a-lock-free-approach-to-parallelizing-stochastic-gradient-descent). Hence, running fastText twice on the same dataset will give different word vectors. One way to remove the randomness is to use a single thread when training the model.

It is also possible to learn a linear transformation to ""align"" the word vectors corresponding to two different runs of fastText (e.g. using the method described in this [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1309.4168)).

16971,IssueCommentEvent,2016-09-09 01:53:47+00:00,53620518,AspenWeb/aspen.py,134455,whit537,17295523,AspenWeb,175831459,245795981,@Changaco What I don't like about your proposal is the `PAGES` object at the bottom. Whether a list or a string, it's ugly and goes against the grain of editors and other tooling, as you suggest. What if, instead, we just admit defeat entirely on mixed-mode files?

In the past, we've toyed with the idea of splitting simplate pages out into a directory. I don't like that because it overrides what a directory means in URL-space (and what would the files inside the directory be named? `foo/foo.html`? `foo/html`?). But separate files in the original directory, on the other hand, would be a natural fit with the way non-dynamic web servers already work. Consider a publishing root with the files:
- if you hit http://example.com/foo.html you'd get the `foo.html` file,

Now, let's say that if you hit http://example.com/foo.py you'd get 404. However, every request for `foo.html` or `foo.json` would consult `foo.py` first, according to the top half of your proposal above. Perhaps we also allow for `foo.{html,json}.py` for processing that is specific to those resource representations (can it import from `foo.py`? but its name may not be Python-safe—perhaps `base` is an injectable dependency?). This should work fine with wildcards; `%foo.html` would consult `%foo.py`.

17037,IssueCommentEvent,2016-10-28 21:36:47+00:00,38581626,pybind/pybind11,4831417,dean0x7d,17565521,pybind,184965859,257034932,"OK, so all concrete Python types now have converting and default constructors which match their Python counterparts. The pytype classes which model protocols (iterator, sequence) did not get any conversions or meaningful default constructors (which should be fine).

The only odd class is `bytes`. There are significant differences between Python 2.x and 3.x which makes a general `object` -> `bytes` conversion pretty messy. There is already a `str` -> `bytes` converting constructor which should be enough for most cases, so I didn't want to complicate this too much.
There were a few things that needed to be adjusted because of the new converting constructors. See the commit messages for details.
Note for the naming of `reinterpret_borrow<T>()` and `reinterpret_steal<T>()`: these are long names, but should serve as warning that there are low-level things.",{},,User,False

17170,IssueCommentEvent,2017-05-23 22:50:55+00:00,26516210,certbot/certbot,6504915,bmw,17889013,certbot,230797895,303554845,"I wrote up the files the different Certbot packages access and what they configure other services to access. I can get into more specifics as necessary.
Certbot needs to be able to do whatever it wants with `/etc/letsencrypt`, `/var/lib/letsencrypt`, and `/var/log/letsencrypt`. This applies to both the Apache and Nginx plugins as well.
The Apache and Nginx plugins largely do the same things to their respective webservers. They need to be able to:
* Read, write, and modify Apache/Nginx configuration files
* Configure Apache/Nginx to include configuration files stored in `/etc/letsencrypt`
* Configure Apache/Nginx to use certificates and keys stored in `/var/lib/letsencrypt` (this is done temporarily for domain validation)
* Copy a file from wherever the plugins Python source files are (e.g. `/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/`) to `/etc/letsencrypt`.

The Apache plugin also currently configures Apache to use a `DocumentRoot` in `/var/lib/letsencrypt` during domain validation, but Apache actually being able to access the files there is unimportant. The only thing that matters is SELinux doesn't cause Apache to blow up.",{},,User,False

17258,IssueCommentEvent,2017-05-10 14:18:33+00:00,58499929,pylti/lti,721299,subssn21,19295652,pylti,167870432,300496543,"I have been working on LTI 2 to get the Registration working. and I ran into some issues I wanted to get comment on:
In LTI2 many Launch Params are gone, including:
context_id, context_title, lis_*, resource_link_title, roles, user_id

It is customary, but not required to use the old names as custom_* when setting parameters in resource handlers in LTI 2. Do we want try and automatically check for custom_ versions of these launch params? Part of me say we should, to keep the rework required for getting params to a minimum. Part of me says we shouldn't since it is a custom and not part of the spec.
One of the issues this creates is there is a function in ToolBase called has_role that depends on the existence of the roles launch param which no longer exists in LTI 2.
There are three ways I can think of handling this:
1. deprecate has_role and its dependent functions and expect users of the library to implement their own role code. (This is what I favor)
2. Leave it and have it return an error if lti_version is 2.
3. Have a way to tell the ToolBase what launch params to check for the role. ",{},,User,False

17348,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-28 14:20:29+00:00,50646767,raiden-network/raiden,3705643,konradkonrad,19666847,raiden-network,274556897,347537451,"> What you think about decreasing the default from 10 seconds to something else?
Not sure; halving it would not hurt. However, the issues can arise from a range of problems, and inspecting the NAT state is hard.
> From the workflows outlined above, participants of the same channel are pinging each other using the public ip / port, that should be sufficient for nat travesal allowing the nodes to communicate.
That is only the case, if the nodes are behind a non-symmetric NAT, i.e. one with fixed port mappings.

One way forward I could see is being more defensive, as in: instead of starting transfers to/via `unknown` network state partners, we could deny them and always wait for a known network state. Users would need to try again themselves after some time. If channel partners can not find each other, we would know that our NAT traversal attempts failed.
However, this would not help for `target` nodes of `unknown` state, because we cannot make target nodes ping us (for invitation) with the current set of protocol messages.

Here our current strategy is to start the health check (for invitation purposes) when the transfer is initiated. If the target is behind NAT, it won't respond to our pings until it learned about our transfer and hence starts the health check to us, the initiator, themselves.",{},COLLABORATOR,User,False

The reason the get_sample_rate function doesn't work for me is that when setting the sample rate the byte to value dict you use in the ATS9870 driver works fine if you aren't using a 10MHz reference signal to generate a sample rate from (which isn't an option for the ATS9870). In the 9360 however if you use the reference signal then you can set any sample rate you want and don't use a byte to value dictionary so unfortunately there can be two ways to map the same sample rate depending on if you use a reference signal or not. Effectively if you use a reference signal you don't want to use a byte to value dict you just send the int rate to the alazar. So no, what you suggest won't solve it but if I sit down and think a bit I can probably come up with a nice way to write it I just didn't have time yet so I left it as a to do.

In terms of acquisition controllers the arraylike parameter works fine for us so far including int he loop (check out the Rohde Schwarz VNA driver whcih has been around a while by now). There are a few plotting bugs but the acquisition part itself is fine. Hence I think it's definitely worth having at least one acq controller which gets an array in qcodes for people to build off. Maybe just the 'basic_controller' I've written gives the idea and together with yours lets people write their own. Let me know what you think when you've had a chance to look. 17741,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-20 22:41:55+00:00,71932349,ray-project/ray,14922,ericl,22125274,ray-project,324676842,390518464,"> We currently only allow one instance type for worker machines, right?

> So the autoscaler needs to be able to detect whether there are tasks that are ready to execute but are prevented from doing so by resource requirements? One subtlety is that tasks could be queued because some object dependency isn't available yet (even if there are enough resources).
That's a good point. I guess the queue length as currently available would not be useful, since you wouldn't know it is blocked on e.g. GPUs.
> It seems like the heuristic of ""check current resource utilization"" should work well as long as the worker instance type is the same as the head node instance type, right?
Yes, or alternatively if the min cluster size has at least 1 worker node.
> Does the autoscaler currently only take into account CPU resources?

It takes into account all types of resources. If any resource's slot utilization exceeds a specified fraction, more worker nodes will be launched. This does assume that launching more worker nodes will actually increase the number of resources of that type.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

18382,IssueCommentEvent,2018-08-27 21:47:09+00:00,33148320,DataBiosphere/toil,8843150,jsmedmar,32805087,DataBiosphere,344900952,416379679,"Want to let you know that #2347 did fix the problem! Nice work @bencvdb, thank you very much. For anyone landing here, I found that in order to get thousands of jobs fast its important to use these flags in combination:
- `--maxLocalJobs`: Set it to a large number, say `2000` or whatever your cluster can support.

- `--statePollingWait`: Use a larger delay for polling wait, this means check on jobs less often. Default is 1 second, that means that the toil worker would be querying the `batchSystem` to often. Do something like five minutes `5*60`. Toil will spend 5 mins submitting jobs instead of submitting **and** checking on jobs.

My last suggestion to toil developers would be to default `maxLocalJobs` to [`maxCpu`](https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/blob/master/src/toil/batchSystems/abstractGridEngineBatchSystem.py#L291). Although further consideration may be needed for that. ",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

A quick primer:  A Pull Request is associated with a particular branch (in this case, your 'master' branch) - any changes you make to that branch will be reflected in the Pull Request.  The force push (`git push -f`) is necessary if you want to push destructive changes, e.g. if you do a `git commit --amend` *after* having pushed the previous commit, as would be necessary here to replace the existing commit with one that did not include the extra files.
Now, since you have two commits - one adding and one removing the files, if you wanted to turn that into one commit without any reference to the extra files, you would do:
1. `git rebase -i`, which opens a file, asking what to do
2. change the `pick` on the second commit (6e8850b) to `squash`, so that it and the previous commit will become one (thus removing any mention of the added/removed files)
3. `git push -f` to push the new state of your branch up to GitHub, replacing what was already there.
I'm happy to make these changes as I merge, but if you want to give it a shot, go for it.
